annotations.label: Random walk

anchor.snippet: a random walk is a stochastic process that describes a path that consists of a succession of random steps

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general random-walk definition; only Brownian motion and CLT-style stochastic objects exist.

annotations.label: Elementary random walk on the integers

anchor.snippet: An elementary example of a random walk is one on the integer number line

annotations.note: No formalization of the integer-line random walk exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lattice random walk

anchor.snippet: A popular random walk model is that of a random walk on a regular lattice

annotations.note: Mathlib has no lattice random walk definition.

annotations.label: Simple random walk

anchor.snippet: In a simple random walk , the location can only jump to neighboring sites of the lattice

annotations.note: No simple-random-walk definition in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Simple symmetric random walk

anchor.snippet: In a simple symmetric random walk on a locally finite lattice, the probabilities of the location jumping to each one of its immediate neighbors are the same

annotations.note: Mathlib lacks any symmetric random walk definition.

annotations.label: Simple bordered symmetric random walk

anchor.snippet: the random walk model is called a simple bordered symmetric random walk

annotations.note: No bordered symmetric random walk in Mathlib.

annotations.label: One-dimensional random walk

anchor.snippet: An elementary example of a random walk is the random walk on the integer number line

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Simple random walk on Z

anchor.snippet: is called the simple random walk on

annotations.note: Simple random walk on Z has no Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Expected position is zero

anchor.snippet: is zero. That is, the mean of all coin flips approaches zero as the number of flips increases

annotations.note: No Mathlib statement about expected position of a simple random walk.

annotations.label: Expected translation distance order sqrt(n)

anchor.snippet: the expected translation distance after n steps, should be of the order of

annotations.note: No √n diffusive scaling result for random walks in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Recurrence of simple random walk on Z

anchor.snippet: a simple random walk on [MATH] will cross every point an infinite number of times

annotations.note: Mathlib does not contain the recurrence theorem for the simple random walk on Z.

annotations.label: Expected hitting time and hitting probabilities

anchor.snippet: the expected number of steps until a one-dimensional simple random walk starting at 0 first hits b or − a is ab

annotations.note: No gambler's-ruin hitting time formula in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Counting walks via Pascal's triangle

anchor.snippet: The number of different walks of n steps where each step is +1 or −1 is 2 n

annotations.note: The underlying identity ∑ C(n,k) = 2^n is in Mathlib, but the random-walk counting interpretation is not.

annotations.label: Central limit theorem for simple random walks

anchor.snippet: The central limit theorem and the law of the iterated logarithm describe important aspects of the behavior of simple random walks

annotations.note: Mathlib has the 1D CLT for iid sums (a generalization), but no law of the iterated logarithm and no random-walk specialization.

annotations.label: CLT and large deviations on crystal lattices

anchor.snippet: it is possible to establish the central limit theorem and large deviation theorem in this setting

annotations.note: No large deviations / crystal-lattice CLT in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Random walk as a Markov chain

anchor.snippet: A one-dimensional random walk can also be looked at as a Markov chain whose state space is given by the integers

annotations.note: Mathlib has Markov kernels but no Markov-chain wrapping of the simple random walk.

annotations.label: Heterogeneous random walk

anchor.snippet: The heterogeneous random walk draws in each time step a random number that determines the local jumping probabilities

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Trajectory is a discrete fractal

anchor.snippet: In fact, one gets a discrete fractal , that is, a set which exhibits stochastic self-similarity on large scales

annotations.note: No statement about discrete fractality of random walk trajectories in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Two-dimensional random walk on a city grid

anchor.snippet: To visualize the two-dimensional case, one can imagine a person walking randomly around a city

annotations.note: Illustrative example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Pólya's recurrence theorem

anchor.snippet: In 1921 George Pólya proved that the person almost surely would in a 2-dimensional random walk, but for 3 dimensions or higher, the probability of returning to the origin decreases

annotations.note: Pólya's recurrence theorem is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Meeting of two independent random walks

anchor.snippet: the difference between their locations (two independent random walks) is also a simple random walk, so they almost surely meet again in a 2-dimensional walk

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Erdős–Taylor intersection theorem

anchor.snippet: Paul Erdős and Samuel James Taylor also showed in 1960 that for dimensions less or equal than 4, two independent random walks starting from any two given points have infinitely many intersections almost surely

annotations.note: Erdős–Taylor intersection result is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rayleigh distribution asymptotic for 2D random walk

anchor.snippet: The asymptotic function for a two-dimensional random walk as the number of steps increases is given by a Rayleigh distribution

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Rayleigh distribution or this asymptotic.

annotations.label: Wiener process

anchor.snippet: A Wiener process is a stochastic process with similar behavior to Brownian motion

annotations.note: Brownian motion (synonymous with Wiener process) is formalized as IsBrownianReal.

annotations.label: Wiener process as scaling limit

anchor.snippet: A Wiener process is the scaling limit of random walk in dimension 1

annotations.note: Scaling-limit theorem (Donsker-type) is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Average steps to hit a circle

anchor.snippet: take a random walk until it hits a circle of radius r times the step length. The average number of steps it performs is r 2

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hausdorff dimension of Wiener trajectory

anchor.snippet: This fact is the discrete version of the fact that a Wiener process walk is a fractal of Hausdorff dimension 2

annotations.note: Hausdorff dimension is in Mathlib but not applied to Brownian sample paths.

annotations.label: Boundary of Wiener trajectory has dimension 4/3

anchor.snippet: the boundary of the trajectory of a Wiener process is a fractal of dimension 4/3, a fact predicted by Mandelbrot using simulations but proved only in 2000 by Lawler , Schramm and Werner

annotations.note: Lawler–Schramm–Werner result is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Skorokhod embedding and KMT coupling

anchor.snippet: The simplest such coupling is the Skorokhod embedding , but there exist more precise couplings, such as Komlós–Major–Tusnády approximation theorem

annotations.note: Neither Skorokhod embedding nor KMT coupling is formalized.

annotations.label: Donsker's theorem

anchor.snippet: The convergence of a random walk toward the Wiener process is controlled by the central limit theorem , and by Donsker's theorem

annotations.note: Mathlib has the finite-dimensional CLT but not the functional Donsker invariance principle.

annotations.label: Gaussian random walk

anchor.snippet: A random walk having a step size that varies according to a normal distribution

annotations.note: Gaussian-step random walk is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Expected value of Gaussian random walk

anchor.snippet: the random walk will vary about a linear trend

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Distribution of translation distance after n steps

anchor.snippet: after [MATH] steps the translation distance's probability distribution is given by

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Root mean square translation distance

anchor.snippet: the root mean square translation distance after [MATH] steps is

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: 68.27% and 50% probability intervals

anchor.snippet: there is a 68.27% probability that the RMS translation distance after [MATH] steps will fall between

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Number of distinct sites visited

anchor.snippet: The number of distinct sites visited by a single random walker

annotations.note: Range of a random walk is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quadratic rate distortion function

anchor.snippet: its quadratic rate distortion function , is given parametrically by

annotations.note: No rate distortion theory in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Random walk on a graph

anchor.snippet: A random walk of length k on a possibly infinite graph G with a root 0 is a stochastic process

annotations.note: Mathlib has combinatorial SimpleGraph.Walk but no probabilistic random walk on a graph.

annotations.label: Transience and resistance to infinity

anchor.snippet: a graph is transient if and only if the resistance between a point and infinity is finite

annotations.note: Electric-network / transience theory is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reversibility of random walk on graph

anchor.snippet: random walk on a graph enjoys a property called time symmetry or reversibility

annotations.note: No reversibility statement for graph random walks in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Random walk in random environment

anchor.snippet: then the random walk is called a "random walk in random environment"

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Maximal entropy random walk

anchor.snippet: in maximal entropy random walk (MERW) we want all paths to be equally probable

annotations.note: MERW is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Self-avoiding walk

anchor.snippet: The self-avoiding walk of length n on [MATH] is the random n -step path which starts at the origin, makes transitions only between adjacent sites

annotations.note: Self-avoiding walks are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Maximal entropy random walk (section)

anchor.snippet: Random walk chosen to maximize entropy rate

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Correlated random walks

anchor.snippet: Random walks where the direction of movement at one time is correlated with the direction of movement at the next time

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Variance of position after n steps

anchor.snippet: A similar calculation, using the independence of the random variables and the fact that

annotations.note: No random-walk variance computation; generic Var of sum exists but not specialized to simple walks.

annotations.label: Gambler's ruin interpretation

anchor.snippet: a gambler with a finite amount of money will eventually lose when playing a fair game against a bank with an infinite amount of money

annotations.note: Gambler's-ruin reasoning is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Probability of S_n = k via binomial coefficient

anchor.snippet: the number of walks which satisfy

annotations.note: Mathlib has Nat.choose but no random-walk-position formula.

annotations.label: Gaussian limit via Stirling's formula

anchor.snippet: taking the limit (and observing that

annotations.note: General iid CLT is formalized; the Stirling-based local-limit derivation for the simple random walk is not.

annotations.label: Probability of recurrence in d dimensions

anchor.snippet: The probability of recurrence is in general

annotations.note: Dimension-dependent recurrence probabilities are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Trajectory of a random walk

anchor.snippet: The trajectory of a random walk is the collection of points visited, considered as a set with disregard to when the walk arrived at the point

annotations.note: Trajectory as range-of-walk is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Variance of random walk position grows linearly in time

anchor.snippet: the walker's position is distributed according to a normal distribution of total variance

annotations.note: For Brownian motion, cov[B s, B t] = min s t gives Var[B t] = t; the discrete random-walk analogue is not separately stated.

annotations.label: Loop-erased random walk

anchor.snippet: The loop-erased random walk

annotations.note: Loop-erased random walk is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reinforced random walk

anchor.snippet: The reinforced random walk

annotations.note: Reinforced random walk is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Random walk hypothesis in financial economics

anchor.snippet: the random walk hypothesis is used to model share prices and other factors

annotations.note: Application/modeling remark with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Pólya's question on meeting of two walkers

anchor.snippet: if two people leave the same starting point, then will they ever meet again?

annotations.note: Pólya's recurrence question and its answer are not formalized in Mathlib.
